title: Social impact investors 'can fix NDIS'
full_text:
Macquarie banker-turned-social entrepreneur Michael Traill is proposing a radical overhaul of the National Disability Insurance Scheme that would result in the mass consolidation of 270,000 providers into a handful of operators run by a syndicate of social impact investors.
Traill, a pioneer of social impact investing who has advised successive governments, is pitching the ambitious idea as a solution to Labor's uphill battle to slash the scheme's runaway growth and achieve $185 billion in critical federal budget savings over a decade.
Social impact investing relies on private capital - from wealthy philanthropists, family offices, foundations and traditional investors such as superannuation funds - to fund infrastructure and social services in sectors including housing, aged care and disability.
The investments can be profitable, generating returns, but they come with a pledge to reinvest a certain amount of capital back into services.
Speaking for the first time about his proposal, which would require backing from superannuation funds and other powerful investors, Traill said it would involve a handful of large disability providers, backed by impact investors, replacing the existing fragmented and mostly unregistered operator network.
"The answer is not more fragmentation. It is fewer, larger, better-resourced providers. The sector needs organisations with the scale to invest in quality systems, retain good staff, absorb compliance costs, fund the technology a modern sector requires and be genuinely accountable for outcomes," Traill said.
Traill, who runs For Purpose Investment Partners, which manages a pool of more than $200 million, said the acquisition he led of the collapsing assets of childcare operator ABC Learning in 2010 by a syndicate of non-profits to create Goodstart Early Learning, as well as recent investments in aged care, were models for the NDIS rescue plan.
He warned that the scheme was at risk of collapsing under its own weight without reform, including an overhaul of its pricing structure and the way the thousands of smaller providers, many of them loss-making, operated. Labor is leaning on ambitious targets to slash NDIS growth to achieve more than half the projected budget savings over the next four years.
Currently, 250,000 unregistered providers and 17,000 registered ones serve 760,000 NDIS participants. Many of them charge aggressively, almost half are loss-making and those that make a profit do not invest their returns back into the system. No provider holds more than 5 per cent market share.
"I support the need for the scheme, but with the visible cost blowouts, it is not sustainable. You could have a number of large-scale, well-run players. You need ethical, well-run players who are there for the long haul, efficient in what they do, who can access capital," Traill told The Australian Financial Review.
"As the government stares into solutions, this is a better, smarter way to think about that."
Traill, who also chairs the Ramsay Foundation, said he was confident about attracting the capital needed for such a large-scale project, but it would require support from superannuation funds.
Australian Ethical, which already invests in disability housing and aged care, supports Traill's idea.
"We already have a couple of investments in the sector, so any initiative which can help us invest more, whether it is in disability housing or impact-type services related to the NDIS, we would be very supportive," Australian Ethical chief investment officer Ludovic Theau said.
"Sometimes the benchmarking arrangement for super funds is not always ideal in this type of sector, so anything we can do at that level to help us invest would be very welcome."
Qantas Super, which has since merged with Australian Retirement Trust, also invested $75 million in For Purpose Investment Partners in 2024.
"The money is there, but what you have to be able to demonstrate is to convince the super funds this is a legitimate, risk-weighted return," Traill said.
It is argued that larger, better-resourced providers would also avoid duplication of finance, compliance, rostering, technology and other administrative functions that thousands of existing providers offer in isolation.
In 2023, Treasurer Jim Chalmers said he wanted to expand impact investment in areas such as aged care, education and disability - but no major deals have materialised.
Traill chaired the federal government's social impact investing taskforce, which made recommendations in 2021 that were never acted on. However, sources close to the discussions but not authorised to speak publicly said the proposals were still under consideration.
"It seemed as though there was going to be a lot of movement and momentum around impact investing, particularly led by the treasurer," said Liz Cameron-Smith, co-CEO of the Foyer Foundation, which provides housing for young people through impact investing.
"We are not seeing there is as much opening up in terms of opportunities, which is disappointing."
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Michael Smith | Health
